item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to have to agree with the comment on the conversational tone. I'm also not sure about how the log is adding to the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't exactly put my finger on it, but something about the D-class's dialogue is off. Still, good article, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tone sounded more conversational than clinical in the testing logs, and the exploration log didn't hold my interest and didn't feel necessary within the context of "It's a huge terrifying spider-wasp what the fuck WHAT THE FUCK"

-1 for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, great image. But I really didn't understand the notes in the Testing Log.

Normally, any conclusions a scientist draws should be based on evidence, in this case, the insects' behavior. More crucially, The results of test 1 should lead to a hypothesis, which results in test 2 being designed to attempt to falsify the hypothesis from Test 1, and so on. You don't always need to state these hypotheses, but you should know what they are. Without this thread of logic, it feels like the scientists are just guessing what the insects' behavior means.

I'm just going to go down the list and say why various bits strained my suspension of disbelief:

+ show block
– hide block

Note: While this may be instinctual behaviour from SCP-4178, it could also be a sign of an expected intelligence level. Further testing with other species has been accepted to expand this theory.

The Hypothesis that they might be more intelligent than other arachnids was a good place to start. But introducing other species into the enclosure wouldn't really make sense as a test of intelligence. IRL, scientists have developed tests to determine that wasps can recognize other wasps' faces, are capable of transitive inference, and that pheromones, not "anger" or "sadness," causes swarms of stinging insects to attack the same animal. It might be worth seeking out an insect specialist in the IRC or the forums, and asking them how to go about testing an insect's relative intelligence.

Note: Compared to the test with the Pompilidae pepsis, this was an unexpected result. It has been requested that the Tarantula Hawk wasp test would be repeated but with a longer time duration than before.

This test pretty much made intuitive sense to me. (If you already tested the spider-wasps with wasps, it makes sense to also test with spiders, right?)

Purpose of the test (test 3): Research SCP-4178's behavior in the presence of a pompilid wasp for a longer duration.

Their surprise that Test 2 didn't work like Test 1 leads to them repeating Test 1 in Test 3 but getting a different result. This was my first hint that something was wrong, but I ignored it and kept reading. I think you could eliminate Test 3 completely by making Test 1 the Goliath Birdeater Tarantulas and Test 2 the Tarantula Hawk Wasps, only it plays out like Test 3. This would eliminate what feels like a redundant test.

Further analysis of the biology of SCP-4178 should be made as soon as possible.

Right. So. What do they do next? They stick a mannequin in the enclosure. Wait, what? What does that have to do with anything?

Just dissect one of them to actually examine its anatomy! Just sedate them, pick one out of the enclosure, and dissect it. If you want to, you could have the insects inside the enclosure start to come to and freaking out as the dissection is going on. Following the movements of the researcher who performed the dissection or ramming into the glass in an effort to stop the dissection. If you really want to go nuts, maybe the one on the operating table comes to as it's being cut open and attacks the scientist, or the other break through the wall of the vivarium, or otherwise breach containment.

Purpose of the test: Research whether SCP-4178 specimens can tell the difference between an inanimate object and a living being.

Regular wasps can't. You know how I know this? I ran a lawnmower over the entrance tunnel to a ground-dwelling yellowjacket's nest once. They stung my foot through my shoe. I went inside the house to put baking soda on it, and one of them followed me inside. As I was treating my sting, I saw it repeatedly stinging my shoe, which was sitting on the floor. Pheremones.

But you don't test this by putting a mannequin in the room and seeing whether or not they try to sting it. Because regular wasps don't sting mannequins unless you've already used that mannequin to smash a wasp of the same species, possibly the same hive. Look at it this way: they sting any object with the blood of dead wasps on it.

I don't think a scientist would test for this. Fortunately, a containment breach or hostile behavior through the glass of the vivarium like I suggested above would reveal this fact to the audience without a test. Actually, I think the fact that they make wasps nests out of corpses demonstrates that they view humans as prey (or at least prime real estate.) But chasing specific humans who've attacked them before, even when they can only see that human through the glass of the vivarium, reinforces their intelligence and almost even proves they're sapient. Make it a sealed, climate-controlled vivarium, and that would mean pheromones are completely uninvolved!

Purpose of the test: Research SCP-4178's behavior toward its ancestor.

Test Subject: Ancestor of SCP-4178's species recovered from SCP-646, labeled SCP-4178-Ω

Note: Soon after the test ended, SCP-4178-Ω was placed in the male vivarium and appears to have been accepted by the other instances. It appears that SCP-4178-Ω's species used to use both pedipalps to prevent sperm competition between males.

I don't really understand why you performed this test, or what we, the audience, were expected to learn from it. I'm assuming the SCP-4178-Ω was born, they quickly DNA tested it, and that's how they determined it was the ancestor of the specific SCP-4178 instances they have in captivity. (I get why the scientists would do it— I just don't get why the writer did it. Maybe I missed the point of this bit.)

Note: Unlike regular insects, it appears that SCP-4178's species is capable of feeling sorrow as well as anger when witnessing the death of a member of its species.

This was the final straw. My suspension of disbelief was shattered at the words "sorrow" and "anger." Unless they cross-tested with some sort of telepathic SCP, I don't see how they could determine that. Again, instances in the vivarium reacting to events outside the vivarium would be a great way to imply this without explicitly stating it.

In conclusion, this is one way you could establish the same properties of 4178, using only three tests, (spider introduction, wasp introduction, 4178 dissection,) with the results of each test leading naturally to the subsequent tests. Unfortunately, I can't help you with test 5 because I don't understand what you were going for with it.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

I hope some of this was helpful. Feel free to use anything I've written here as inspiration. If you do decide to make changes, even if you take it in a different direction, please feel free to message me about it. I would love to see where it goes from here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How can they contact the wiki to confirm ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You put together two of the most terrifying things with a great story. The exploration with the D-Class was pretty neat with the ancient temple. Although I liked they made it out alive, I would have liked to see that at least one D-Class got stung or bit. Don't know why though, probably just to see if being stung / bitten by the bug would have some different effects than just a venomous bite.

I thoroughly enjoyed it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I commissionned the image from an artist I know, I can ask them to send a message to confirm it if you wish

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted at the image alone and the rest of the article simply secured that. God damn dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did have measurements for the instances actually, but a couple of people I showed the draft to pointed out it wasn't necessary and to leave atleast a hint of their size (which I did with the Meganeura monyi comparaison early in the description). To be frank I'm not happy with the exploration too, I'll look into shortening it in the future. Thanks for taking the time to review the article though ^^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a few problems that made me dislike this article.

The exploration log, to me, was too long, and it didn't keep my interest. I'm not sure that the temple itself was important to the overall story of the article.

My main problem with the article was that I don't really see how the species is anomalous. Big flying arachnids with above-average intelligence are weird, but they don't do anything outside of mainstream reality, unless I'm missing something. Also— we don't get a good idea of how big the creatures are, (again) unless I missed something.

The concept might be lost on me because I'm not that scared of the creepy-crawlies, but I think that my analysis stands regardless.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hahahahaha, no thank you kindly please. Take your upvote damn you, and I'll sit here and hope my nightmares are kind to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spiders AND wasps? Both of my phobias? In ONE SCP??
+1

item.title: The Ol' Switcheroo
item.content:encoded: I like how the Class-D's didn't just follow the cookie cutter formula and die horribly at the end of the exploration log. It's a refreshing change of pace that they made it out alive and virtually unscathed.
